coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots-wrapped vertically aligned zinc oxide nanorods arrays for photosensing application
coredata.dc:creator: Kathalingam, A.
Salman Ajmal, Hafiz Muhammad
Vikraman, Dhanasekaran
Kim, Sam-Dong
Park, Hyun-Chang
Kim, Hyun-Seok
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we report an innovative and facile approach to prepare graphene quantum dot (GQD)-wrapped vertically aligned zinc oxide (ZnO) nanorod (NR) arrays. The ZnO NR/GQD heterostructure was prepared as a core/shell structure on an indium tin oxide (ITO) substrate using spin-coating, electron-beam evaporation, and a rapid thermal process combined in a novel way. Spin-coated poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) was converted to GQDs on ZnO NRs by high temperature thermal treatment. A metal-capping layer was used to convert PMMA into GOD by avoiding the thermal evaporation of PMMA. Two different metals Ni and In were used as capping layers and studied their effect in the thermal conversion of PMMA into GQDs and in the properties of the fabricated devices. Structural, morphological, compositional and optical properties of the as-prepared ZnO NR/GQD heterostructures were characterized. The devices consisting of 50–70 nm ZnO NRs covered with GQDs as a core-shell structure demonstrated efficient UV photosensing with excellent photoresponse due to the GQDs showing high carrier transport and photoabsorption. The fabricated ITO/ZnO NR/GQD/In device showed better photosensing responsivity than the ITO/ZnO NR/GQD/Ni one. This is an entirely new technique for growing GQDs directly on previously prepared ZnO NRs.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots-wrapped vertically aligned zinc oxide nanorods arrays for photosensing application

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we report an innovative and facile approach to prepare graphene quantum dot (GQD)-wrapped vertically aligned zinc oxide (ZnO) nanorod (NR) arrays. The ZnO NR/GQD heterostructure was prepared as a core/shell structure on an indium tin oxide (ITO) substrate using spin-coating, electron-beam evaporation, and a rapid thermal process combined in a novel way. Spin-coated poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) was converted to GQDs on ZnO NRs by high temperature thermal treatment. A metal-capping layer was used to convert PMMA into GOD by avoiding the thermal evaporation of PMMA. Two different metals Ni and In were used as capping layers and studied their effect in the thermal conversion of PMMA into GQDs and in the properties of the fabricated devices. Structural, morphological, compositional and optical properties of the as-prepared ZnO NR/GQD heterostructures were characterized. The devices consisting of 50–70 nm ZnO NRs covered with GQDs as a core-shell structure demonstrated efficient UV photosensing with excellent photoresponse due to the GQDs showing high carrier transport and photoabsorption. The fabricated ITO/ZnO NR/GQD/In device showed better photosensing responsivity than the ITO/ZnO NR/GQD/Ni one. This is an entirely new technique for growing GQDs directly on previously prepared ZnO NRs.

ce:section.ce:para: Oxide semiconducting materials with wide bandgaps, such as zinc oxide (ZnO), tin dioxide, indium tin oxide (ITO), etc., are commonly used in optoelectronic devices due to their excellent optical and electrical properties [1]. The nanostructures of these oxide materials such as nanowires, nanorods (NRs), nanotubes, and quantum dots (QDs) have attracted immense interest because of their exciting physical, chemical, and size-tunable optical properties. One-dimensional (1D) nanostructures can produce improved charge generation and conduction suitable for photo-active applications [2]. Among the various oxide materials, ZnO nanostructures play an interesting role due to their wide bandgap (3.3 eV) and high excitonic binding energy (60 meV) at room temperature [3,4]. Although these properties are useful for various applications, they are particularly useful for optoelectronic devices such as solar cells and photosensors, as well as in photocatalytic applications [5]. Moreover, ZnO NRs with their large surface-to-volume ratio have attracted wide attention for various sensor applications, including ultraviolet (UV) photodetectors [6]. However, the challenge for the use of ZnO in photosensors lies in avoiding the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs [7]. Surficial electron-hole recombination can be avoided by modifying the surface of ZnO NRs with QDs to improve charge collection and transport [8]. The QDs adsorbed onto the ZnO NRs improves the efficiency by modifying the ZnO bandgap and other optical properties. Besides photo-induced charge separation, charge transport can also be improved by photoexcitation. QD-sensitized nanomaterials have emerged as potential candidates for various applications [9]. Lead sulfide (PbS), cadmium sulfide (CdS), and cadmium-selenium (CdSe) QDs are popularly used for the enhancement of photosensitivity of nanostructures [10]. Similarly, graphene QDs (GQDs) have recently gained attention for various applications [11,12].

Graphene, a unique two-dimensional (2D) material of carbon atoms, has also been very much in the limelight recently for use in various optoelectronic applications because of its versatile and astounding properties of high mechanical strength, excellent electron conductivity, electrical mobility, and optically tunable properties [13,14]. Graphene has unique characteristics that are useful for a variety of applications [15]. As it is very sensitive to its surrounding environment, it can be used as an efficient material for various sensor applications [13]. The incorporation of quantum structures with the ZnO nanostructures facilitates efficient separation of charge carriers upon photon excitation. That is, the incorporation of GQD with ZnO can utilize the incident light efficiently and produce large number of electron-hole charges. Moreover, the adsorbed GQD act as a passivation layer, and due to its enhanced conduction property, it can reduce the charge recombination due to the defects on ZnO NR. Hence, the surface defects are effectively passivated due to the adsorbed GQD [7,16]. Recently, Lien et al. reported the use of graphene as an efficient transparent photodetector [17]. Graphene can be made as 3D graphite, 1D nanotubes, 0D fullerenes, or QDs. These nanodimensional allotropes of carbon play an important role in today’s research. GQDs are single or a few layers of graphene with a size of a few nanometers that exhibit outstanding optical and electrical properties because of plasmon-induced effects [18,19]. In particular, the size-dependent bandgap and high optical absorption of GQDs are very useful for photosensing [20]. Moreover, their tunable Fermi energy levels can promote surface plasmon propagation, thereby increasing the photosensitivity of the composite structure.

Nanocomposites of graphene and oxide material hybrids have recently surfaced in various technological important devices including optoelectronic devices [9,21]. The high solubility, good biocompatibility, stable photoluminescence (PL), high specific surface area, high mobility, and tunable bandgap of GQDs make it a promising candidate for various applications [22]. In recent years, much attention has been given to GQDs due to their unique electro-optic properties. Their bandgaps can also be tuned by chemical modification [23]. Moreover, GQDs show more pronounced edge effects compared to other QDs [22,24]. These qualities have triggered interest in nanolevel device fabrication using GQDs and ZnO NRs in hybrid structures. The decoration of this novel 2D material onto ZnO NRs can boost the efficiency of optoelectronic and other sensor devices [25]. ZnO is a very useful material for UV light-sensing applications because it absorbs largely in the UV region due to its wide bandgap (3.32 eV) and large surface-to-volume ratio [26]. Therefore, GQD-decorated ZnO NRs can boost the latter’s UV photosensing property. Recently, a number of reports have been made available on ZnO/graphene-based photodetectors [27–29]. However, the decoration of GQDs onto vertically aligned ZnO NRs is a tedious process, and so there are not many reports on the growth of GQD-decorated vertically aligned ZnO NR devices as core/shell structures for UV-sensing applications. Moreover, the sensitivity of the prepared ZnO NR/GQD hybrid device depends on various factors during the preparation of the ZnO NR/GQD heterostructure to ensure good alignment between the materials. In general, both materials are synthesized separately and then mixed using different techniques. Spin-coating is generally used for the incorporation of graphene or related materials onto vertically grown ZnO NR arrays [30]. The GQDs prepared separately via different routes are coated onto the already prepared ZnO NRs by spin-coating. In general, carbon-based materials such as GQDs are prepared by decomposing organic polymers, e.g. evaporating organic precursors such as poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) as the carbonaceous source followed by controlled chemical vapor deposition (CVD) of the graphene layers. At high temperature, the polymer chain is rearranged to form graphene layers via sp2 hybridization. The CVD-prepared graphene layers are of high quality and can be used to make devices by transferring the formed layers. In contrast, a graphene powder prepared via Hummers’ method can be spin-coated for the preparation of graphene-based hybrid films. Similarly, spin-coating is used for the preparation of the ZnO NR/GQD core/shell hybrid devices. However, as a novel and innovative approach in this work, we directly converted the PMMA to GQDs coated on ZnO NRs via a facile rapid thermal process (RTP). High temperature thermal annealing can convert the PMMA layer into carbon and graphene layers. However, simple hotplate annealing at open atmosphere is not feasible to convert the PMMA into graphene, on the other hand it evaporates the PMMA. Instead of the hotplate annealing, the rapid thermal annealing (RTA) is found to be useful for the conversion of PMMA into graphene as reported in our previous work [31]. Because, the conversion of PMMA into graphene needs high temperature fast annealing and cooling provided with a metal cover layer to avoid evaporation. In RTA processing, the polymer layer is heated to a high temperature shortly in a highly controlled way. Moreover, it flashes heat radiation for a short duration on the surface of the film (several nanometers) without affecting the interior of the film at controlled gas ambient condition. Hence, a rapid thermal annealing process was selected for the high temperature annealing and thermal conversion of PMMA into graphene in this work.

This method yields a good wrapping of ZnO NR with GQDs and/or other carbon-based materials depending on the process parameters. High temperature RTP annealing of PMMA with a metal cap layer can convert the PMMA into graphene as we reported in our previous work [31]. It has been found that the adsorbed GQDs improve the photosensing property of the ZnO NRs by plasmon-induced high carrier transport. Spin-coating methods may not result in perfect adhesion of graphene materials with ZnO NRs, but this is not the case in the proposed method as it directly converted onto ZnO NRs. In addition, the improved thermally induced adherence results in significant enhancements in the interfacial properties of the hybrid. For the thermal conversion of PMMA into GQD and to avoid evaporation a metal-capping layer was used. Two different capping layers such as Ni and In were used by spin-coated and studied their effects on the photosensing property of the devices. As UV detection is requisite for a wide range of applications in the military, civil, medical, environmental, biological sensor fields, this report will attract various groups of researchers.

Analytical grade pure chemicals purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA) were used in the fabrication of ITO/ZnO NR/GQD/Ni and ITO/ZnO NR/GQD/In structured devices. Prior to the deposition of the different layers, the ITO substrates were cleaned well by sonication in acetone and isopropanol for 10 min each. Afterward, they were washed in deionized water followed by nitrogen blow-drying. The growth of ZnO NRs on a clean ITO substrate was carried out using a two-step hydrothermal process based on our previous works [32–36]. In the first step, a seed layer was formed on the substrate by spin-coating a solution consisting of 10 mM zinc acetate dehydrate in 1-propanol at a rate of 3000 rpm for 30 s. A portion of the ITO substrate was masked using adhesive tape for the purpose of probe contact. Five spin-coating cycles were applied to form a sufficient seed layer thickness. The spin-coated substrates were hot-baked for 1 min at 100 °C after each spin-coating cycle to evaporate the solvent and enhance the adhesion of the seed layer. After completing the required spin-coating cycles, the ZnO seed film was annealed at 350 °C for 1 h. In the next step, the ITO substrate with the seed layer was kept immersed in the ZnO precursor solution consisting of 25 mM zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2.6H2O) and hexamethylenetetramine (HMTA) at 90 °C for 5 h. Finally, the substrates with the vertically grown ZnO NRs layer were removed from the solution after a set period, rinsed in DI water, and dried using blown nitrogen gas.

For the preparation of the GQD-decorated ZnO NRs, the PMMA was spin-coated onto the grown ZnO NRs and thermal treated. The PMMA (molecular weight 850,000 g/mol, Sigma-Aldrich) dissolved in toluene in the volume ratio of 1:4 was spin-coated onto the pre-formed ZnO NRs at 3000 rpm for 30 s. Next, the PMMA-coated ZnO NRs were annealed at 110 °C for 10 min to remove the solvents. The PMMA layer coated on the ZnO NRs was masked with a metal layer of either Ni (50 nm thick) or In (75 nm thick) to examine the effect of a metal-capping layer on the conversion of PMMA into graphene and on the device properties. An E-beam evaporator of Korea Vacuum Model KVE-T5560 was used for the deposition of metal-capping layer on PMMA layer. The samples were first placed on the substrate-dome with a sticky-tape and metal pellets in the crucible inside the vacuum-chamber. Then closing the chamber, a high vacuum (less than 10−5) was produced inside the chamber. After attaining the required pressure, the current for the e-gun was increased slowly to attain the required materials evaporation rate. The metal was heated until to reach desired thickness of the metal coating. During deposition, the metal deposition rate was controlled by electric and magnetic fields. Metal deposition rate of 1 kA/s and thickness were monitored and controlled using a crystal type thickness meter until reaching the required thickness of metal. Finally, the chamber was opened and removed the sample for further processing. The metal-capping layer-coated ZnO NR/PMMA devices were annealed via RTP at ∼600 °C for 10 s to form GQD wrapped ZnO NRs structure. For the controlled heating and conversion of PMMA layer coated on the ZnO NRs, a RTP system (KVR-020, Korea vacuum) was used. It uses a convenient, efficient and fast reacting thermal source made up of tungsten-halogen lamps with easily controllable heating and cooling arrangements. It has a software program interfaced with thermocouples and sensors to monitor and control the chamber temperature and time. The Ni/PMMA@/ZnO NRs coated ITO substrate was placed on the sample holder of RTP chamber. To sense and control the temperature of the samples, the required data were fed into the system, and using the installed program the sensors were precisely controlled to attain the temperature and time. After the set period (∼10s) at 600 °C, a nitrogen gas was allowed into the chamber to cool down for the removal of the device. The thickness of PMMA layer and metal capping layers were changed to study the thickness effect in the conversion of PMMA into graphene. The whole device fabrication process is depicted in the schematic diagram in Fig. 1 .

The structural, compositional and optical properties of the fabricated ITO/ZnO NR/GQD/Ni (device 1) and ITO/ZnO NR/GQD/In (device 2) structures were characterized using X-ray diffraction (XRD), field emission-scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), energy dispersive X-ray analysis (EDX), transmission electron microscope (TEM), UV-Vis spectroscopy, laser Raman spectroscopy, Photoluminescence (PL) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) studies. The surface morphology and compositional analysis of the fabricated composites were conducted using FE-SEM (S-4800, Hitachi, Kyushu, Japan) with an attached EDX set-up, JEOL-2010 F transmission electron microscope and PHI 5000 Versa Probe II X-ray photoelectron spectroscope. A LabRam Aramis laser Raman spectrometer (Horiba, Kyoto, Japan) using an argon ion laser with an excitation wavelength of 532 nm was used for the Raman spectroscopic study. PL spectra of the devices were obtained using a luminescence spectrometer (LS-55, PerkinElmer, Waltham, MA, USA) with an excitation wavelength of 325 nm. Photocurrent variation of the devices was measured using a 2611B source meter (Keithley Instruments, Cleveland, OH, USA) under different illumination and bias conditions. For UV light illumination, a UV light source (300W Xenon arc lamp) with a power density of 140 μW/cm2 at 360 nm wavelength was used.

The properties of the fabricated ITO/ZnO NR/GQD/Ni and ITO/ZnO NR/GQD/In hybrid structures were characterized. As the transparent conducting oxide materials are usually post-annealed to improve their conductivity and transparency properties, the prepared devices were also RTP-annealed to simultaneously convert the PMMA into graphene and to improve the conduction properties of the hydrothermally prepared ZnO NRs.

Typical FE-SEM images of ZnO NRs and the ZnO NR/GQD heterostructure grown on an ITO substrate are shown in Figs. 2 and 3
, respectively. Fig. 2 displays a dense array of uniform ZnO NRs with an average size of around 50 nm grown vertically on an ITO substrate. All of the ZnO NRs show perfect hexagonal-shaped top ends that indicate pure crystalline ZnO. The cross-sectional view in the inset of Fig. 2 shows quasi-aligned ZnO NRs with a height of around 600 nm. Fig. 3(a,b) and 3(c,d) presents top-view and cross-sectional views of the ITO/ZnO NR/GQD/Ni and ITO/ZnO NR/GQD/In devices, respectively, each with an inset showing a magnified view of the respective image. The In-coated surface shows thicker crystalline particles deposited on the grown ZnO NR layer compared to the Ni-coated one. The cross-sectional views clearly show the presence of GQDs on the ZnO NR surface, mostly on the top part of the NRs. As the In or Ni layer is coated as clustered islands or individual particles, as shown in the FE-SEM image, it induced the formation of small pieces of graphene from the PMMA layer below the metal islands and evaporating the remaining PMMA in between the metal islands. Hence, very small pieces of graphene (GQDs) were formed instead of a large continuous layer. Fig. 4 displays EDX spectra of the ITO/ZnO NR/GQD/In device indicating the presence of Zn, O, In and C confirming the formation of expected composition without any other impurities.

TEM analysis was also performed to further examine the structural and interfacial properties of Ni/ZnO NRs/GQD heterostructures. Fig. 5 shows the typical TEM images of GQD and Ni particles adsorbed ZnO NRs. As seen from the images, the GQD and Ni/NiO particles are attached on the ZnO NRs surface. The lattice fringe width observed equal to 0.28 nm attributes to the (002) planes of ZnO as displayed in Fig. 6 . The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of this region indicates the c-axis oriented growth of ZnO NRs. The lattice fringe width∼0.24 nm and 0.21 nm observed in the highlighted region correspond to the (111) and (200) planes of NiO respectively, its corresponding SAED pattern indicates the polycrystalline nature of NiO [37]. The region circled in Fig. 6 indicating a d-space value as 0.21 nm denotes the (100) planes of graphitic carbon signifying the presence of GQD. These diffraction rings shown in SAED pattern attributes the crystalline quality of the formed product [38].

XRD patterns were obtained to analyze the crystalline properties of the as-prepared ZnO NR/GQD/Ni and ZnO NR/GQD/In composite nanostructures (Fig. 7 (a) and (b), respectively). For the ZnO NR/GQD/Ni device, the peaks observed at 2θ = 31.7°, 34.5°, 36.3°, 47.6°, 56.4°, 62.9°, 66.9°, 89.6°, and 98.8° corresponding to the (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103), (112), (203), and (114) planes indicate the formation of hexagonal wurtzite crystal structure ZnO NRs (Fig. 7(a)) [39]. These values confirm the growth of hexagonal wurtzite ZnO with lattice parameters a = 3.24 Å and c = 5.25 Å. Moreover, the high intensity of the (002) peak indicates c-axis orientation signifying the vertically aligned growth of ZnO NRs. The peaks found at the 2θ values of 44.34°, 51.72° and 76.26° correspond to the (111), (200), and (220) planes of face-centered cubic Ni coated over the ZnO NR/PMMA layer [40], while the peaks located at about 2θ = 62.8°, 76.07°, and 79.38° are due to the respective (220), (311), and (222) planes of nickel oxide (NiO) [41,42]. The weak and superimposed diffraction peaks obtained at around 22.5°, 44.5°, 49.8°, 51.6°, 53.6°, and 81.6° indicate the respective (002), (101), (100), (004), (102), and (112) hexagonal graphitic planes of graphite (JCPDS file no. 41–1487) [43–45], which confirm the presence of graphene in the fabricated structure.

In the case of the ZnO NR/GQD/In structure, the (002), (101), (102), (110), (103), (112), (203) planes of the ZnO NRs appeared except for the (100) plane present in the ZnO NR/Gr/Ni structure (Fig. 7(b)). This missing plane peak could have been superimposed by one of the indium oxide (In2O3) peaks; the In2O3 layer was formed due to the thermal treatment induced conversion of the In layer in the presence of ZnO. The respective peaks located at 21.58°, 30.47°, 37.6°, 41.89°, 45.74°, 50.88°, 60.68° are indicating the (112), (222), (114), (323), (314), (044), and (226) planes of cubic bixbyite-type polycrystalline In2O3 (Card No. 04-005-9883) [46]. The respective peaks appearing at 2θ = 26.48° and 51.71° due to the (002) and (004) crystalline planes of graphene confirm its formation [39]. However, the peaks at around 12° in the ZnO NR/GQD/Ni structure and 16° in the ZnO NR/GQD/In structure indicate the formation of graphene oxide [47]. Compared to the Ni-coated device, the In-coated one shows extra peaks for carbon due to the interaction of In2O3 with graphene. Moreover, the increased PMMA thickness can obviously produce extra phases. The peaks at 2θ = 16°, 24°, and 25.5° indicate the mixed phases of graphene, graphene oxide, and amorphous carbon [48,49]. As the PMMA/ZnO composite was subjected to thermal treatment, the inclusion of oxygen during the conversion of PMMA into carbon is also obvious. There is a significant change in the intensity and position of the graphitic peaks obtained for the ZnO NR/GQD/In structure compared to the ZnO NR/GQD/Ni structure, which can be attributed to the effect of In and ZnO on graphene during the thermal annealing process [50]. The other unidentified peaks are due to a different form of graphitic carbon, as reported by Chaoyu et al. [51]. Hence, the XRD analysis confirms the growth of the ZnO NR/GQD/Ni and ZnO NR/GQD/In structures.

The optical properties of the fabricated devices were analyzed using UV-Vis and PL spectroscopy. Fig. 8 shows the transmission spectra of pristine ZnO NRs, and the ZnO NR/GQD/Ni and ZnO NR/GQD/In composite structures, in the wavelength range of 200–1000 nm. The pristine ZnO NR film displays low transmittance and high absorption from 200 to 300 nm. However, for wavelengths longer than 400 nm, the transmittance is linearly increased with the increase in wavelength, thus signifying poor absorbance at higher wavelengths. Both ZnO NR/GQD/In and ZnO NR/GQD/Ni composite devices showed an optical absorption peak with a sharp edge in the 295–370 nm region. Although this absorption in the UV region is obviously due to ZnO [52], it is different from the pristine ZnO NRs, indicating the effect of the GQDs on UV absorption. Meanwhile, the pristine ZnO NR film shows an absorption edge diffused below 350 nm, which is different from the graphene-incorporated devices and indicates the effect of the GQDs on the absorption efficiency of the composites. Both the ZnO NR/GQD/Ni and ZnO NR/GQD/In GQD hybrid devices show enhanced absorption extending to longer wavelengths, thus indicating a bandgap decrease [9], as well as three UV-Vis absorption bands with a shoulder at approximately 295 nm due to the graphitic structure. The weak absorption obtained at around 370 nm is due to n/π∗ transitions of the C=O bonds [53,54]. Compared to the Ni-covered structure, the In-coated one showed high transmittance even though the metal layer was a little thicker. Moreover, the In-coated device shows strong absorption peaks at about 300–370 nm and 625 nm which we believe to be from the GQDs.

In Fig. 8, the plots (i) of each device are for thicker PMMA layer compared to the plots (ii) which shows decreased transmittance indicating the increased absorption of the devices with more GQD. The transmittance increase of Ni-coated device was only 5% for the change in the PMMA-layer thickness whereas for the In-coated film increased by nearly 12% due to the high transmittance of the In top layer. The In-covered devices showed much more pronounced absorption in the UV region (300–370 nm) than the Ni-covered devices. This higher UV absorption is indicative of the suitability of the In-coated devices for UV photosensing applications. Moreover, the incorporation of GQDs enhanced the efficiency of UV light absorption by both metal-coated devices [20] indicating localized surface plasmon-induced absorption due to the size- and shape-dependent variation of the GQDs. The sharp rise in absorption at 296 nm and persistence up to 370 nm indicates excitonic absorption by the ZnO NRs [48]. The In coating on PMMA and annealed at high temperature affected the graphene formed by reacting with ZnO and thin In2O3 interlayer created between the In and graphene. Moreover, the In could have caused In–O bonding in the ultrathin interface. The In2O3 interlayer formed by the high-temperature RTP was also highly transparent. Moreover, this high-temperature environment could have aided for the formation of In-doped ZnO, which is also a highly transparent and conductive interlayer [55]. Fig. 8(d) shows the Tauc plots obtained for the prinstine ZnO NRs, In/GQD/ZnO NRs and Ni/GQD/ZnO NRs using transmittance data. The bandgap of the structures are found to decrease from 3.07 eV (pristine ZnO) to other structures as 2.88 eV for In/GQD/ZnO NRs and 2.65 eV Ni/GQD/ZnO NRs.

As Raman spectroscopy depends on light and matter interaction it can help to clarify environmental and surficial changes of materials [55]. Hence, Raman spectra of the grown structures were obtained to examine the vibrational modes of the composite nanostructures and to confirm the composition of the formed structures using laser excitation of wavelength 532 nm. Fig. 9 shows Raman spectra of the ITO/ZnO NR/GQD/Ni structures made with thin and thick PMMA layers. Raman scattering by the thicker PMMA layer-coated structure displayed in Fig. 9(a) shows enhanced peaks compared to the too thinner PMMA-coated devices in Fig. 9(b), thereby indicating the dominant vibrational mode of graphene produced by the sp2 carbon atoms vibration of the transverse optical (TO) and longitudinal optical (LO) phonon modes. Usually, this is characterized by the D, G, and 2D band peaks at around 1350, 1580, and 2700 cm−1, respectively. The D band is aroused due to the disorder formed by the sp2 hybridization of carbon atoms, while the G band is the graphene layers’ first-order scattering of the E2g stretching mode vibration [23]. The devices prepared with thick PMMA layers (Fig. 9(a)) also show standard peaks for a graphene layer at around 1350, 1595, and 2708 cm−1 for the D, G, and 2D bands, respectively, indicating the presence of graphene in the fabricated device. Besides, there were extra peaks at 79, 155, 244, 485, 2440, 2935, and 3195 cm−1: the latter three peaks 2440 cm−1, 2935 cm−1 and 3195 cm−1 also denote the wrinkled or turbostratic structures of defective graphene layers whereas the other peaks are due to the ZnO and Ni layers. Compared to the thicker PMMA layer-coated devices, those prepared with thin PMMA layers show a number of extra peaks located at 442, 570, 730, 906, and 1090 cm−1 assigned to 1P (TO) 1P(LO), 2P (2TO), TO + LO, 2LO phonon modes of NiO, respectively [56–58]. This NiO interlayer formed between Ni and ZnO layers due to thermal treatment is more pronounced in the thinner PMMA sample. The peaks located at 101, 380, 407, 437, 574, and 583 cm−1 were labeled as E2 (Low), A1(TO), E1(TO), E2 (High), A1(LO) and E1(LO), respectively, indicating the wurtzite hexagonal phase of the ZnO NRs, which is in good agreement with previous reports [59]. Besides, some acoustic and optical phonon overtone peaks were also observed at 200, 1100, and 1702 cm−1 [60].

The thinner PMMA-coated device shows more peaks, indicating defects and Ni-doping of the ZnO, than the thicker PMMA-coated where the prominent graphene peaks hid the other bands [30,52]. The Raman band obtained at around 1780 cm−1 was aroused due to the C–O vibration mode, which indicates the synergetic attachment of graphene to the ZnO [48]. All of the peaks were slightly shifted compared to standard values, which is attributed to disorder created due to the interaction of the ZnO and Ni layers and the high-temperature treatment.

Generally, all-carbon systems show three prominent Raman bands as are also present in traditional graphene sheets obtained via the popular CVD method [39]. However, graphene sheets produced via alternative and new routes can have other more unusual bands due to the surficial changes produced by the associated environment. Therefore, the graphene produced via our method shows additional bands at 2445, 2690, 2925, and 3160 cm−1 besides the usual D, G, and 2D bands, indicating the defects (wrinkles) and surficial modifications created due to the interaction of ZnO and the top layer under high-temperature RTP. Moreover, the broadened and overlapped D and G peaks indicate defective and the disordered orientation of the graphene sheets. In accordance with the literature, the 2445 and 2690 cm−1 bands were assigned to the G∗ and 2D (G′) Raman modes, respectively [61] while the two peaks in the far high wavenumber region at 2925 and 3160 cm−1 were assigned to the D + D′ or D + G and 2D’ (G + D′) modes, respectively [62]. The increased intensity of the D peak compared to the G peak and other unusual peaks are indicative of defective GQDs formed as wrinkled and misaligned very small graphene sheets as clusters of QDs formed on the surface of the ZnO NRs. The G band was split into two sub-bands, as shown in the inset of Fig. 9(b), signifying the strain produced on the graphene due to the Ni/NiO particles. This G band split and shift quantifies the charge transfer taking place between the graphene and the adjoining compounds [37]. The intensity ratio of the D and G bands (ID/IG) was higher than 1, indicating disordered graphitic layers. Compare to the thick PMMA-layer device (1.04), the thin PMMA-layer device shows a higher (1.24) ID/IG value that signifies a more defective graphitic layer due to interactions between the ZnO and NiO layers. Compared to pure and defect-free graphene layers, defects such as wrinkled and overlaid (or turbostratic) graphene layers can result in improved photoresponse [26]. This defective nature of graphene indicates the successful incorporation of graphene onto the ZnO.

A PL study is a useful characterization technique to understand the optical emission properties of the fabricated ZnO NR/GQD/top-metal hybrid nanostructures [58]. The room-temperature PL spectra of the fabricated devices obtained in the region 350–800 nm are given in Fig. 10 , which indicates UV, green, and near-infrared (NIR) emissions. Both the Ni- and In-covered samples show the same profile but with small shifts toward the higher wavelength region for the In-coated device. The bands obtained at around 379 and 532 nm were attributed to the excitonic recombination (near-band edge emission) and deep-level or trap-state emissions, respectively [63]. The other weak band located at approximately 756 nm in the NIR region could be due to interstitial oxygen defects, as reported by Ravi et al. [26].

Generally, ZnO emits in the green band along with its characteristic UV emission. The induced visible and NIR PL emission is of interest for trying to understand the properties of the ZnO NR/GQD/top-metal structures and their suitability for various applications. NIR PL has rarely been reported, especially for ZnO NR-graphene hybrid nanostructures [23,26]. The mechanism behind the broad visible and NIR emissions is still not clearly understood. The NIR PL emission of the hydrothermally grown ZnO NRs obtained at ∼750 nm can be attributed to the transition between Zn and oxygen vacancies and/or the radiative recombination of shallow-trapped electrons and deep-trapped holes, while the green band can be explained by the GQD-covered ZnO NRs arising from the Zn interstitials and/or the carbon-doped ZnO. Wang et al. [64] showed green emission from graphene due to the transition between π and π∗ bands. Although the graphene is a zero-bandgap material, GQDs can develop a bandgap due to interfacial defects produced by ZnO and quantum confinement effects [29]. The PL properties of the graphene layer can also be affected by variation in the thickness. Similarly, a change in the area of the ZnO covered by GQDs can also alter the bandgap. The coating of GQDs around the ZnO NRs can alter the bandgap due to strain change and formation of C–O and Zn–C bonds causing charge transfer in the interface between the ZnO and GQD [13]. Compared to the Ni-coated devices, the In-coated ones show enhanced PL emission both in the UV and visible regions, thus indicating its suitability for photosensing applications. Generally, in nanostructures the surface passivation reduces the charge traps induced by surface defects protecting the structure from ambient conditions. Hence, the surface passivation can improve the electrical and optical properties of the nanostructures with enhanced luminescence by avoiding the non-radiative recombination and by healing the surface defects [65]. However, depending upon the surface passivated compounds and its nature the luminescence can be either increased or decreased because the vibrational states of O1s and Zn 2p can be changed due to the adsorbed compounds. The green emission obtained for ZnO nanostructures is believed that mostly due to the defect states of ZnO [66]. Here, in our case the indium covered GQD/ZnO NRs device shows high intensity of both peaks UV and visible due to the luminescent GQD. This is because of the smooth and thin cover of GQDs around the ZnO NRs, whereas this effect is less in case of Ni covered devices. As the PMMA coating thickness is high, it might not be produced smooth and thin GQD cover causing a thick graphitic carbon. In this case, the thicker graphitic and NiO particles adsorbed on the ZnO NRs can trap the photogenerated hole-electron pairs, and reduced the recombination causing lower photoemission [67].

X –ray photoelectron spectroscopy was also carried out to inspect the elemental composition of the Ni/GQD/ZnO composite structure. The full survey spectrum of Ni/GQD/ZnO NRs structure displayed in Fig. 11 (a) indicates the presence of Zn, O, C, and Ni in the prepared films. The survey spectrum revealed Zn 3d, Zn 3p and Zn 3s etc., peaks along with Zn 2p peaks. Fig. 11(b) shows the Ni 2p spectrum of the composite, which displays a strong peak at 854.1 eV indicating the metallic Ni [68]. The weak peak observed at 855.8 eV indicates the nonlocal screening effect by the NiO formed. It also shows two satellites peaks at 861 eV and 879.7 eV belonging to Ni–O bonds [69]. The deconvoluted peaks obtained at 529.6 eV and 531.4 eV (Fig. 11(c)) indicates respectively the states of O1s spectrum [25,70]. Fig. 11(d) displays two peaks at 1021.7 eV and 1044.6 eV indicating the Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2 states of ZnO. The difference of binding energies between the Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2 states is equal to 23.0 eV, which further indicates the formed ZnO [68,71]. The deconvoluted peaks of C1s peak is shown in Fig. 11(e), which display three binding energy peaks at 284.7 eV, 286.4 eVand 288.3 eV, corresponding to the C–C, C–O and C=O bonds, respectively. This confirms the presence of GQD on the surface of ZnO NRs [38]. These results justify the formation of Ni/GQD/ZnO NR structure in this work.

Current-voltage (I–V) responses of the fabricated devices were studied with and without illumination to analyze their photosensing properties. Fig. 12 (a) and (b) shows I–V plots of the Ni-coated device measured under different illumination conditions using tip-based probes, as shown in the inset of Fig. 12(b). The I–V plots under dark and room conditions are reproduced in Fig. 12(b) to highlight the nature of the I–V response. The non-linear I–V nature of the device signifies the formation of junctions with a barrier between the ZnO NRs and the metal coated on them. A drastic increase in current under illuminated conditions denotes the photosensitivity of the device. This Ni-covered ZnO NR/GQD structure produced a higher current under green light (550 nm) illumination than under UV light illumination (360 nm) whereas the In-covered device (Fig. 12(c)) showed enhanced current under all illuminated conditions with an especially high current under UV light illumination. This current level In covered device is more than 10 times that of pristine ZnO NRs, as shown in Fig. 12(d), indicating the efficient charge collection and transfer of the GQD-coated ZnO NRs with a top In layer. That is to say, under illuminated conditions, the charge recombination is minimized due to enhanced charge mobility and the conduction by the GQDs increasing the current. That is, the In-coated device produces high current than the Ni-covered device under the same condition. As stated earlier, the Ni-coated device produced less current under UV light irradiation than under 550 nm light (green) illumination, whereas the In-coated device showed higher current under UV illumination than under 550 nm light, thereby demonstrating the significance of the In the UV photosensing effect of In-covered devices. Upon UV light irradiation, more electron-hole pairs are generated; thereby increasing the carrier concentration, and consequently, the current in the device is increased due to high transmittance of In or In2O3. This denotes the suitability of In-coated ZnO/GQD structure for UV photosensing applications.

As shown in Fig. 14, a highest responsivity of 45.71 A/W for UV (360 nm) is obtained compared to other visible light wavelengths, clearly showing the efficiency of the fabricated device for UV photosensing. The In-coated device showed no significant response for other light wavelengths and showed only UV photosensing. Although this UV photosensing is due to the suitable bandgap of ZnO (3.3 eV), the Ni-coated device cannot sense UV light because of the opaqueness of Ni top layer, whereas the transparency of In the visible region allows UV light photons through and excites electrons from the valence band to the conduction band, thereby producing electron-hole pairs and consequently, photocurrent. For the UV photosensing of the GQD-coated ZnO NR structures with a metal top layer, the surface defective states are more beneficial for the generation of electron-hole pairs by light photons. Moreover, compared to bulk or particulate ZnO, the 1D NR film is efficient due to its high surface-to-volume ratio and quantum confinement effects [55] and its large surface area is prone to producing more electron-hole pairs. The schematic diagram in Fig. 13(b) presents the mechanism of photocurrent generation. As soon as the light is incident on the interface between the GQD and ZnO NR electron-hole pairs are generated. The generated holes are trapped by the oxygen-trap centers and the unpaired free electrons move to the electrode producing current. A combination of the hydrothermal preparation-induced defects and GQD-induced defects along with the formed In2O3 produced large trap centers that boosted the electron population and photocurrent [73]. However, the observed current is less, approximately in micro ampere range at 1 V bias. This lesser current may be due to the poor charge collection efficiency of tip-based measurements.

The resultant GQD wrapped ZnO NRs devices exhibited significant photosensitivity with fast time. This tiny GQD covered on the ZnO NRs can respond efficiently for the photoexcitation due to the quantum size effects. This loaded quantum dots can have number of weak coordination bonds causing unexpected effects leading to improved sensing properties. These GQD structures can have many functional and coordination groups at their edges forming π conjugated structures, which can produce efficient absorption sites [38]. Compared to ZnO NRs devices the GQD/ZnO NRs devices show improved photosensing effect highlighting the advantage of GQD in this composited structure. The improvement in photocurrent of the GQD adsorbed ZnO NRs device attributes the enhanced photoabsorption and carrier transport of the heterostructure [74].

In the metal tip-based measurements, current conduction is produced through the metal electrodes via the ZnO NRs, where only a few of ZnO NRs are involved in the conduction process because, as can be seen in the FE-SEM image in Fig. 3(a), the metal coating (50–55 nm thick) on the ZnO NRs is not continuous. Therefore, instead of metal tip-based measurement system, a sandwich-type parallel transparent plate ITO was used as shown in Fig. 15 (a) inset.

Surprisingly, the current is increased enormously in both the devices as shown in Fig. 15(a), and so it is obvious as this sandwich-type conductor simultaneously collects the charge carriers from all of the ZnO NRs. Fig. 15(a) displays I–V plots of the Ni- and In-coated devices in the sandwich-type configuration under room-illumination conditions. Similar to the tip-based measurements, the In-coated devices produced higher photocurrent than the Ni-coated ones in sandwich type also. The devices’ current levels were tested in the original and inverted structures, as shown in the inset of Fig. 15(b). Therein, the I–V plots of Ni-coated device measured under room-illumination conditions show a higher current for the inverted structure. This difference could be due to the Ni metal coating on the ZnO NRs because it can impede the entrance of light into the device due to its less transparence than In metal. However, more light incident to the ZnO NRs is permitted in the inverted configuration via the highly transparent ITO substrate. This face-dependent variation is only pronounced in the Ni-coated device, whereas, no change is observed in the In-coated devices, attributing the high transmittance of In metal cover. It can be seen that the In-coated device is highly transparent, as indicated by the cross-mark drawn at the bottom in the insets of Fig. 15(d).

Fig. 15 (c,d) shows I–V plots of the devices in the sandwich-type configuration under different illuminated conditions. They are showing high current with linear variation compared to the metal tip-based configuration, which indicates the simultaneous collection of current from all the vertically aligned ZnO NRs. In addition, the In-coated device showed a high sensitivity for UV light, inferring that the In coating that used to avoid PMMA evaporation improved the UV photosensing in this device configuration. The metal coated on the PMMA and the annealing at high temperature affected the formation of the graphene, and the thin In2O3 interlayer formed between the ZnO and In influenced the properties of the formed GQDs. The high-temperature RTP induced the formation of an In2O3 interlayer that was highly transparent. The bonding between In and O formed in the ultrathin interface also induced more oxygen-trap centers and active edges in the GQDs. Moreover, the high-temperature RTP aided the formation of In-doped ZnO (ZnO:In), which is also a highly transparent and conductive interlayer [75]. Hence, the defect-induced charge trapping centers present on the surface of the ZnO NRs largely enhanced the UV photosensing. The graphene adsorbed on the ZnO NRs can increase the mobility of the created free charges and increase the photosensing property of the heterostructure. Due to the interaction of the GQDs with the oxide material, sub-energy states are created which influence the excitonic energy states in the interface [76].

Cyclic I–V scans of the devices were also conducted for the applied potentials between −2 V and +2 V in the tip probe configuration to further probe the effect of top metal cover. They demonstrated hysteresis loop-like I–V plots, as shown in Fig. 16 (a and b), suggesting that the fabricated devices act as memristive switches due to the ZnO NRs. The cyclic I–V scan for the ITO/ZnO NR/GQD/Ni device under room-illumination conditions in the range −2 V to +2 V is shown in Fig. 16(a). The increase in current in the reverse scan indicates the switching of the device from the high resistance state to the low resistance state. That is to say, for the initial scanning 0 V to +2 V the device was in the high resistance state (OFF), and it was switched to the low resistance state (ON) during the reverse scan from +2 V to 0 V. The ON state was maintained until the reverse bias was applied, which caused it to switch to the OFF state. The inset in Fig. 16(a) shows a reproduced I–V plot in the range of −1 V to +1 V that clearly distinguishes the ON and OFF states of the device, showing that the switching between the two resistance states is well-resolved. This phenomenon under cyclic scanning indicates that the device typically acts like a memristor. Fig. 16(b) shows the cyclic scan of the ITO/ZnO NR/GQD/In device, indicating a less pronounced memristive effect. Meanwhile, the sandwich-type configuration did not show the memristive property, which indicates an individual ZnO NR-dependent switching effect. It is believed that this resistive switching of the devices is due to the formation and rupture of conducting filaments formed in the interface between the metal and oxide layers. That is to say, during the SET process (high-to-low resistance switching), a thin conducting metal filament is formed, and in the RESET process (low-to-high resistance switching), the formed metal filament is ruptured due to the opposite potential [75]. This switching property is more pronounced in the Ni-coated devices than the In-coated ones.

In order to observe the top metal dependent catalytic effect cyclic voltammograms (CVs) of the devices were studied. The cyclic voltammograms of the devices recorded in the potential range −2 V to +2 V in 1 M KOH solution is shown in Fig. 16(c and d). As shown in Fig. 16(c), the Ni-coated device shows prominent redox peaks compared to the In-coated device with increased current. This indicates that the ZnO NR/GQD/Ni structure can act as an efficient electrode for electrochemical applications. The devices were also subjected to I–V characterization after CV measurements to witness change produced in the film due to electrochemical process. The I–V plots of the ITO/ZnO NR/GQD/Ni and ITO/ZnO NR/GQD/In devices measured using tip-based configuration are shown in Fig. 17.

The devices are found to yield higher current levels both in the dark and under UV illumination conditions than before CV measurements. Both showed higher current levels under UV irradiation irrespective of the top metal used, which is unlike the previous (pre-CV) measurements. The current levels are also higher with linear variation. It is interesting to note that the CV cyclic measurement improved the device structure and optical sensing efficiency. This infers that the CV cyclic action filled the spaces between the ZnO NRs with bulk deposition, which improved the charge conduction. The ZnO also could have been doped with K ions, which can also improve the optical sensing of ZnO [76]. In addition, due to the effect of the electrochemical action, the top metallic layer was depleted, which increased the transparency of the device. Fig. 18 shows FE-SEM and EDX results of the ITO/ZnO NR/GQD/In device after CV cyclic measurement. As shown in Fig. 18(a), the top surface is corroded by the removal of the In layer; the cross-sectional FE-SEM image shows thick grain-like structures fully covering the ZnO NRs. The EDX pattern and elemental composition also confirmed the removal of the In layer and the addition of K in the device composition. In a nutshell, we can say that the CV cycling experiment improved the device’s photosensing activity. In the electrochemical cell and within the solution, the adsorbed particles could easily be liquefied and deposited at the bottom of ZnO NRs under the stress of the potential fully covering the space between the NRs, whereas in spin-coating and electron-beam evaporation, it is difficult to penetrate deep into the ZnO NRs and deposit particles at the bottom. This phenomenon poses a number of interesting questions and opens doors for further exploration in this area. We are also interested in further investigating the effect of CV cycling on the device structure.

Novel ZnO NR/GQD-based core/shell heterostructures with a top metal layer fabricated using spin-coating, E-beam evaporation and RTP combined strategy were studied for UV photosensing application. SEM, XRD, laser Raman, PL, and other studies confirmed the incorporation of the GQDs in the fabricated devices that improved the photocurrent generation by the enhancement of carrier mobility reducing recombination under UV light illumination. The ZnO NR/GQDs/In devices presented an excellent UV photosensing response with a responsivity of 45.71 A/W. Indium metal covered device showed superior response on photosensing compared to Ni metal covered devices. Ni metal covered devices showed enhanced memristive and electrocatalytic performances. In current collection techniques sandwich-type parallel plate conductor showed excellent charge collection with high current compared to tip-based metal contact probe, Tip-based measurements showed potential-dependent switching properties at low biases. Cyclic voltammetry redox experiment of the fabricated heterostructure devices improved the efficiency of the devices for photosensing by modifying their structures. The In-metal covered device is suitable for photosensing applications in this devices configuration, whereas, the Ni metal covered devices is suitable for photocatalytic and memristive applications.

These findings of this study offer the opportunity to further explore this novel fabrication technique, different phases of graphene structures and top metal dependent applications such as photosensor with In metal, and memristive and catalytic applications with Ni metal.

A.
